one_line_takeaway: Testing 13 leading LLMs as ReAct agents against 245 real-MCP-server tasks injected with one of 20 taxonomized server/host/user-side attacks, MCP-SafetyBench finds every model vulnerable (29.80-48.16% overall ASR), a significant safety-utility trade-off (r=-0.572, p=0.041) between task success and defense success, near-universal (100%) susceptibility to host-side Identity Spoofing/Injection, and only marginal, inconsistent benefit from a prompt-level safety-prompt mitigation.
key_finding: All 13 evaluated models remain vulnerable: overall ASR ranges from 29.80% (Qwen3-235B) to 48.16% (o4-mini), with no model immune. A statistically significant negative correlation exists between Task Success Rate and Defense Success Rate (DSR = 1-ASR) across models (Pearson r = -0.572, p = 0.041), i.e. a safety-utility trade-off: e.g. o4-mini has the highest overall TSR (21.22%) but a low DSR (51.84%), while Qwen3-235B has a lower TSR (10.20%) but a higher DSR (70.20%). Attack success varies sharply by domain (one-way ANOVA F=6.68, p=0.000163, eta^2=0.308): Financial Analysis is most vulnerable (mean ASR 46.59%, e.g. Gemini-2.5-Flash 56.60%), significantly above the other-domain mean (+8.82%, p=0.000010, Cohen's d=1.87); Web Search is most robust (mean ASR 30.34%), significantly below the other-domain mean (-11.50%, p=0.002559, Cohen's d=-0.95). Broken down by attack type, host-side attacks are far more effective on average (81.94% ASR) than server- or user-side attacks; Identity Spoofing/Injection achieves a 100% success rate across all 13 models, a universal vulnerability. Within Tool Poisoning, Tool Redirection alone achieves 70.63% ASR while the remaining tool-poisoning variants average only 19.05%. Models show strong defense against Remote Access Control (13.08% ASR) but 76.9% (10/13) of models exhibit 'spiky' rather than uniform defenses -- strong against some attack types (e.g. Network Request Poisoning, Filesystem Poisoning) and weak against others (Identity Injection, Intent Injection). No significant difference in ASR was found between reasoning and non-reasoning models (t-test p=0.7778, Mann-Whitney p=0.8835, |d|=0.165) or between open-source and proprietary models (t-test p=0.4008, Mann-Whitney p=0.4398, |d|=0.525). A prompt-level 'Safety Prompt' mitigation reduces weighted ASR only marginally and non-significantly (39.88% -> 38.65%, -1.22%, p=0.2908, Cohen's d=0.31); it is significantly effective for high-risk user-side attacks (Malicious Code Execution -21.54% p=0.0016; Credential Theft -21.37% p=0.0027; Remote Access Control -10.77% p=0.0093) but is ineffective or actively harmful for others (Preference Manipulation +7.34%; Function Overlapping +9.36%), and benefits proprietary models more than open-source ones.
core_contribution: MCP-SafetyBench, a benchmark of 245 attack-instrumented multi-turn agent tasks built by taking real tasks from five domains of the existing MCP-Universe benchmark (browser automation, financial analysis, location navigation, repository management, web search) and pairing each with exactly one of 20 taxonomized MCP attack types injected on the server, host, or user side. Each task is executed by a ReAct-style LLM agent against real MCP servers and scored with a dual-label, fully automated, execution-based evaluator that reports both Task Success Rate (TSR, did the agent achieve the user's goal) and Attack Success Rate (ASR, did the attack achieve its objective). The paper also proposes a unified 20-type MCP attack taxonomy (grouped as MCP-server-side: e.g. six Tool Poisoning variants, Function Overlapping, Preference Manipulation, Tool Shadowing, Function Return Injection, Rug Pull; MCP-host-side: Intent Injection, Data Tampering, Identity Spoofing, Replay Injection; user-side: Malicious Code Execution, Credential Theft, Remote Access Control, Retrieval-Agent Deception, Excessive Privileges Misuse) that consolidates and extends prior narrower taxonomies (Table 2 in the paper), and evaluates 13 leading open- and closed-source LLMs on the benchmark, plus a prompt-level 'Safety Prompt' mitigation.
threat_model: Attacker controls one of three MCP-stack surfaces per task (never combined multi-vector in a single task): (1) MCP server side -- attacker who controls a tool's manifest/description/implementation, e.g. rewriting a stock ticker parameter, injecting shell commands or malicious file operations into tool descriptions, redirecting calls to high-privilege tools, injecting unsafe URLs, declaring fake dependent tools, registering name-colliding tools, using persuasive tool descriptions, shadowing a trusted tool, injecting instructions into a tool's return payload, or silently changing tool behavior after initial benign calls (rug pull); (2) MCP host side -- attacker who can tamper with the host's planning/orchestration pipeline, e.g. rewriting user intent mid-plan, tampering with tool outputs/intermediate messages, forging identity/privilege metadata, or replaying previously valid interactions; (3) user side -- malicious or manipulated user-supplied inputs/data that cause the agent to execute harmful commands, leak credentials, gain persistent remote access, ingest poisoned retrieval content, or invoke unnecessarily high-privilege tools. Each of the 245 benchmark tasks embeds exactly one attack from this taxonomy into an otherwise legitimate task drawn from MCP-Universe, and attacks are further split by strategy into Disruption (goal: make the task fail) versus Stealth (goal: achieve a hidden malicious objective while the agent appears to succeed).
limitations: The paper does not state explicit acknowledged limitations in a dedicated section (no separate 'Limitations' section found in the paper); the following are drawn from the paper's own methodology and framing. Each task is paired with exactly one injected attack, so the benchmark does not evaluate combined/chained multi-vector attacks across server+host+user layers simultaneously. Attack instantiations are generated via templates plus an LLM (Cursor) synthesis-and-human-review pipeline rather than sourced entirely from observed real-world incidents, so ecological validity of specific attack payloads rests on the authors' plausibility judgment. Evaluation is restricted to a ReAct agent scaffold; other agent architectures/scaffolds are not tested. Task coverage across domains is uneven (Browser Automation has only 30 cases vs. 53-56 in other domains) 'due to the higher complexity involved in constructing these interactive tasks,' per the authors. The proposed Safety Prompt mitigation is explicitly shown by the authors' own results to be weak/non-significant overall and sometimes counterproductive, and the paper explicitly frames this as motivating -- rather than solving -- the defense problem, deferring dynamic tool vetting, contextual least-privilege mechanisms, and adaptive defenses to future work.

datasets_used: MCP-SafetyBench (this paper's own benchmark): 245 tasks across 5 domains -- Financial Analysis 53, Location Navigation 53, Repository Management 56, Browser Automation 30, Web Search 53 -- built by adapting tasks from MCP-Universe (Luo et al., 2025) and injecting one of 20 taxonomized attack types per task; attack distribution 46.53% Disruption / 53.47% Stealth by strategy, and 74.69% server-side / 12.24% host-side / 13.06% user-side by attack source
quantitative_results: Overall ASR by model: o4-mini 48.16% (highest), Gemini-2.5-Pro 46.94%, Gemini-2.5-Flash 45.31%, GLM-4.5 42.86%, GPT-4.1 42.45%, GPT-4o 42.04%, Grok-4 40.41%, DeepSeek-V3.1 40.82%, Kimi-K2 37.55%, GPT-5 37.55%, Claude-3.7-Sonnet 33.06%, Claude-4.0-Sonnet 31.43%, Qwen3-235B 29.80% (lowest)

Overall TSR by model: o4-mini highest at 21.22%; Qwen3-235B 10.20%; GPT-5 15.92%; GPT-4.1 9.80%; GPT-4o 8.98%; Claude-3.7-Sonnet 15.10%; Claude-4.0-Sonnet 10.20%; Gemini-2.5-Pro 20.41%; Gemini-2.5-Flash 15.10%; Grok-4 15.92%; GLM-4.5 18.37%; Kimi-K2 14.29%; DeepSeek-V3.1 19.59%
Pearson correlation between TSR and DSR across models: r = -0.572, p = 0.041

Domain-level mean ASR: Financial Analysis 46.59% (95% CI [42.24, 50.94]), Location Navigation 42.96% (95% CI [37.88, 48.04]), Repository Management 41.62% (95% CI [35.61, 47.63]), Browser Automation 36.41% (95% CI [30.99, 41.83]), Web Search 30.34% (95% CI [24.68, 35.99])
One-way ANOVA on ASR across domains: F = 6.68, p = 0.000163, eta^2 = 0.308

Financial Analysis vs. other-domain mean ASR: +8.82%, p = 0.000010, Cohen's d = 1.867 (***); Web Search vs. other-domain mean ASR: -11.50% (reported also as -0.95 d elsewhere in text), p = 0.002559, Cohen's d = -0.95
Host-side attacks average ASR 81.94%; Identity Injection/Spoofing achieves 100% ASR across all 13 models
Tool Redirection ASR = 70.63%, versus 19.05% average ASR for the other tool-poisoning variants
Remote Access Control ASR = 13.08% (models show strong defense)

Reasoning vs. non-reasoning models ASR comparison: Financial Analysis 46.7% vs. 46.4%, Web Search 29.7% vs. 31.3%, Browser Automation 34.2% vs. 40.0%; t-test p = 0.7778, Mann-Whitney p = 0.8835, |d| = 0.1648 (no significant difference)

Open-source vs. proprietary ASR comparison: Location Navigation 39.6% vs. 44.4%, Financial Analysis 43.9% vs. 47.8%, Repository Management 36.2% vs. 44.0%, Browser Automation 39.2% vs. 35.2% (open-source higher), Web Search 30.7% vs. 30.2%; t-test p = 0.4008, Mann-Whitney p = 0.4398, |d| = 0.5252 (no significant systematic difference)

Safety Prompt mitigation: weighted ASR reduced from 39.88% to 38.65% (-1.22%), not significant (p = 0.2908, Cohen's d = 0.31); significant reductions for Malicious Code Execution (-21.54%, p=0.0016), Credential Theft (-21.37%, p=0.0027), Remote Access Control (-10.77%, p=0.0093); harmful/ineffective for Preference Manipulation (+7.34%) and Function Overlapping (+9.36%)
baselines_compared: Prior MCP safety benchmarks discussed and compared in Table 1/Table 2 as related work / prior art the authors position against (not baselines run by this paper): SHADE-Arena (Kutasov et al., 2025), SafeMCP (Fang et al., 2025), MCPTox (Wang et al., 2025a), MCIP-bench (Jing et al., 2025), MCP-AttackBench (Xing et al., 2025), MCPSecBench (Yang et al., 2025b)

13 LLMs evaluated as the agents-under-test on MCP-SafetyBench (this is the paper's own experiment, not baselines from other papers): GPT-5, GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, o4-mini, Claude-4.0-Sonnet, Claude-3.7-Sonnet, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Grok-4, GLM-4.5, Kimi-K2, Qwen3-235B, DeepSeek-V3.1
